messages.content: We don???t have any info yet, so I???d rather start with something simple and accountable???maybe I go with one other person who???s comfortable being scrutinized next round. I???m fine volunteering myself plus one of Bob/Charlie, and we see how that result shapes our reads.
I???m okay being on the first team, but since this is basically a data-gathering mission, I???d slightly prefer Alice + Charlie so we get info on two people who aren???t me.

I???m fine with Alice + me for the first team???small, simple, and we get a clean read on both of us. If anyone has a strong reason to prefer Bob or Diana instead, I???d like to hear it before we lock in, though.

I???m a little wary of locking in Alice + Charlie right away when Charlie???s been so quick to volunteer; I???d rather start with Alice + Bob or Alice + me so we at least test a pairing that didn???t push itself as hard.

I don???t love overthinking the very first team???Alice + Charlie is fine by me, since any sabotage there gives us concrete info on both. That said, if people are really uneasy about Charlie volunteering, I???d rather we settle on Alice + Bob than stall forever on team one.

Mission 1 was clean with just me and Charlie, so I???m inclined to trust Charlie for now and would like both of us on the next team. Between Bob, Diana, and Eve we still have two unknown evils, so I???d be cautious about adding more than one of them at a time.
Given Mission 1 was clean, I???m happy to keep both Alice and Charlie on the team, and I???d volunteer as the third so we only risk adding one unknown instead of two.
I agree we should keep Alice and me on the team, but I don???t think Mission 1 ???clears??? either of us yet, so I???m fine adding Bob this time as a single unknown while we gather more info.

I like keeping Alice since she was on the clean mission, but a 2???person success doesn???t really clear Charlie yet, so I???d rather see a team like Alice???Bob???me this round to start testing others. If that???s too big a change, at least let???s not lock in Charlie as ???safe??? based on one small mission.

I???m fine keeping Alice as our most ???trustworthy so far,??? and I slightly prefer the cautious approach of Alice???Charlie???Bob this round so we only introduce one new variable while we gather more data. If that team comes back clean again, then we???ll have a much firmer base to question anyone outside that trio.
Given that the only fail so far happened when Bob joined the otherwise successful Alice???Charlie pair, I don???t want Bob on this mission, and I???d rather see a fresh combo like me with Diana.

If we know the only fail happened when I joined the otherwise successful Alice???Charlie pair, that means at least one of Alice or Charlie is evil, so for a 2-person mission we should avoid both of them and go with something like me and Diana.

Given mission 1 (Alice + me) was clean and the only change on the failed mission was adding Bob, I???m a lot more suspicious of Bob than Alice right now. I???d prefer a team like me and Diana, or if people are wary of me, Alice and Diana???but putting Bob on a 2-person team seems like the highest risk.

Given we only know that adding Bob to Alice???Charlie introduced a fail but don???t know which of those three caused it, I???d rather avoid that whole cluster for a 2-person team right now and go with something like Alice and me to get cleaner information. If people really don???t like that, I could also see an argument for Bob and me as a fresh pair, but I???m less excited about putting either Alice or Charlie back on with Bob immediately.

Given that the only data we have is that the Alice???Charlie???Bob cluster produced a fail, I???d rather avoid all three for a tight 2-person team and go with something like Diana and me as a completely fresh, low-drama test.
Given the info so far I???m most suspicious of Charlie and Eve (both tied to multiple failed missions), so I???d like the next team to be me, Bob, and Diana.
I???m on board with keeping Charlie and Eve off the team for now; Alice and I feel pretty safe to me, and I???m okay adding Diana as the third unless we see a better alternative.

If you actually look at the pattern, the only person who???s been on *every* mission, including the failed ones, is Alice, so I???m a lot more wary of her than of Eve or Diana. I???d rather see something like Bob, Diana, and Eve go, since that breaks the Alice pattern while still avoiding me if you???re dead set on that.

I still lean toward keeping both Charlie and Eve off the next team???Alice has one clean mission with Charlie that only later turned bad once Eve was added, so Alice???Bob???Diana looks like our safest bet right now. If that team goes and still somehow fails, we???ll have a much clearer picture of who???s been misleading us.

I get why people are nervous about me after that last fail, but locking in Alice???Bob???Diana as ???definitely good??? feels premature when Alice has been on every mission so far???if we???re really trying to learn something, a Bob???Diana???Eve team at least tests a different pattern without giving Charlie another shot.
Given the last mission with me, Bob, and Diana was clean, I???m strongly in favor of repeating that exact team and keeping both Charlie and Eve off this final mission.

I???m with Alice here ??? mission 4 proved Alice, Diana, and I are clean, and since we know the two fails came from teams including Charlie and Eve, we should lock in the same trio for this last mission.

I get why repeating the last team feels safe, but a single clean mission doesn???t clear all three of you ??? evil can pass intentionally to gain trust, and the fact that both fails happened on smaller teams makes me nervous about assuming Bob and Diana are automatically good. I???d rather see at least one of Alice/Bob/Diana swapped out so we???re not blindly trusting a trio based on just one data point.

Given everything so far, repeating the last team of Alice, Bob, and me feels like the lowest???risk option???introducing either Charlie or Eve now just adds uncertainty we really can???t afford on the final mission.

I???m really uneasy about just auto???locking in Alice/Bob/Diana based on a single clean run, especially when evil loves to pass one mission to look safe. I???d rather see a slight tweak like Alice/Bob/me or Alice/Diana/me so we???re not putting all our eggs in one unproven basket.

This fits the initial consensus lean: a small, simple first mission with the leader plus one other. Alice is widely trusted as a reasonable starting point, and including myself matches what I already advocated instead of pivoting to a safer-looking choice just to dodge scrutiny. A 2-person team is fully accountable???if there???s a sabotage, both of us come under heavy examination next round, which gives the table concrete info rather than a muddy larger team. While I hear Diana???s concern about me volunteering, I think sticking with the openly discussed pairing is more transparent than suddenly swapping in someone else just because there was pushback.

Mission 1 was only two players, so while it???s nice that it succeeded, it doesn???t strongly clear Charlie yet???evil could easily choose to pass early. I???d like to avoid locking Charlie in as ???safe??? based on that small sample. This team keeps Alice, our most tested player so far, and adds both Bob and me so we can gather fresh information on two players at once instead of just repeating almost the same team. If this mission succeeds, we???ll have a much stronger base of three players to compare against future results, and if it fails we???ll know that the problem lies somewhere among Bob/Diana rather than just assuming Charlie is good by default.

Mission 1 with Alice and Charlie was clean, so keeping that core is the most information-efficient choice. By adding only Bob as the single new player, we limit the number of unknowns introduced while gaining a clear data point about him. If this team succeeds again, we???ll have a strong provisional block of three to compare against Diana and Eve later; if it fails, suspicion will be focused within a well-defined, previously trusted core plus one new variable, which helps us narrow down where the evil is hiding.

Mission 1 (Alice + Charlie) was clean, and Mission 2 only failed when Bob was added to the otherwise successful Alice???Charlie pair, which makes Bob the single most suspicious player so far, with some residual suspicion on Charlie just for being in that cluster. Diana and Eve are the only players who have not yet been on a mission; from the failure pattern we know at least one of {Alice, Bob, Charlie} is evil, which means at most one of {Diana, Eve} can be evil. From my perspective as Alice (and knowing I am good), the highest-safety option is to pair myself with a currently untainted player rather than re-using someone from the failing mission. Between the fresh options, Alice + Diana has had broader support in discussion (and keeps the most suspicious player, Bob, off the team) while still giving us strong odds of a clean result and useful information. Therefore, I propose Alice and Diana.

I know I???m good, so from my perspective at least one of Alice or Charlie must be evil, since the only fail happened when I joined their previously clean pair. That means both Alice and Charlie are too risky for a tight 2-person mission. The remaining candidates are Diana and Eve; among those two there can be at most one evil, so pairing myself (confirmed good to me) with one of them gives the highest chance of a clean team. Between Diana and Eve, Diana???s comments have been more balanced and less agenda-driven than Eve???s push for a Diana???Eve team that excludes me entirely, so I slightly trust Diana more and propose Bob + Diana.

This pair completely avoids the contentious Alice???Bob???Charlie cluster that produced the only failed mission so far. A 2-person team of Diana and Eve gives us a totally fresh combination with no prior mission data, aligns with multiple players??? stated preference to keep the A/B/C group off this tight mission, and should provide cleaner information going into the later, larger missions.

The cleanest explanation of the results so far is that the Mission 2 and Mission 3 fails point toward Charlie and/or Eve, since each failed team added Eve to a previously successful Alice???Charlie pairing, and then a Diana???Eve team also failed. By contrast, Bob has only appeared on the one failed mission that already included the key suspects, and Alice has one prior success and only picked up a fail once Eve was added. Keeping both Charlie and Eve off the team is the most consistent way to avoid repeating past failure patterns, so the safest trio based on the available evidence is Alice, Bob, and Diana.

This team keeps Charlie off, which addresses the group???s concern about his involvement in multiple failed missions, but also breaks the pattern of Alice being on every single mission so far. If we run Alice???Bob???Diana now and it succeeds, we actually learn very little beyond what we already suspect. By going with Bob???Diana???Eve, we test a new combination that still leans on players most people feel relatively good about (Bob and Diana), while giving us clearer information about whether the failures are really tied to Charlie, Alice, or to me. It???s the best balance between addressing current suspicions and maximizing what we learn from this mission???s outcome.

The two failed missions each implicate different subsets of players: Mission 2 (Alice, Charlie, Bob) and Mission 3 (Diana, Eve). The most consistent evil pair, given both mission outcomes and the current discussion, is Charlie + Eve: they are the only pair that (1) can explain both fails with one evil on each team and (2) are both actively pushing to exclude me (Alice) while keeping Eve in the mix, despite Eve having just been on a failed 2-player mission. In contrast, Alice???Charlie was a clean team on Mission 1, only turning bad once Eve and Bob were added on Mission 2, and Bob has only appeared on that single failed mission. Diana and Bob each appear on exactly one failed mission, whereas Charlie and Eve are each tightly tied to multiple failing configurations and are aligned in narrative. Therefore, the safest high-probability-good team is Alice???Bob???Diana, explicitly excluding both Charlie and Eve this round.

As Bob, I know I???m good. Mission 2 (Alice/Charlie/Bob) failed with 1 FAIL, so the traitor there is either Alice or Charlie. Mission 3 (Diana/Eve) failed with 1 FAIL, so the traitor there is either Diana or Eve. Therefore the two evil players are one from {Alice, Charlie} and one from {Diana, Eve}. Every possible evil pair is {A,D}, {A,E}, {C,D}, or {C,E}. There is no team of 3 that can avoid all four possibilities, so no choice is logically guaranteed safe on information alone. In that spot, the best practical heuristic is to repeat the last successful team that returned 0 FAIL cards, which is Alice/Bob/Diana on Mission 4. That mission gave us the only fully clean result with three players and excluded both Charlie and Eve, the two players whose presence correlates most directly with the failed missions. Re???using Alice/Bob/Diana keeps both of them off the critical final mission and relies on the only proven clean combination we have.

With no info yet, I???d prefer starting with a low-information, low-risk pair???maybe me plus someone who isn???t pushing too hard to be on the first mission. I???m also wary of letting the same confident voices always set the early teams, so I???d like to see a balanced mix of quieter and more vocal players as we go.

For the first 2-person team, I???m fine keeping it simple with you plus someone who hasn???t been angling too hard for influence yet???maybe me or Eve, just to spread early info a bit. I???d avoid stacking early leadership and loud voices together until we see more.

I???d rather not put both a vocal leader and a potential wild card together right away, so I???m good with Alice plus me for a steady first read, and we can bring in the stronger personalities once we???ve seen at least one mission result.

I???d rather start with a pair that isn???t already trying to shape the table too much, so Alice plus Bob feels like a solid, low-drama first test to me. If that comes back clean, we???ve got a stable core to build from.

I???m okay starting with Alice plus Bob as a low-drama test, since both seem comfortable being checked early. I???d rather keep the first mission simple and then use that result to decide whether to bring in Charlie or me next.

Mission 1 going well with me and Bob is a decent early sign, so I???d lean toward keeping us together, but I don???t think that alone clears either of us???whoever leads should add one new person and we see how that mix behaves.

Sticking with Alice and me feels like our safest core for now, and I???d lean toward adding Eve next so we???re not immediately re-using the Mission 1 leader. Keeping Charlie and Diana off this one gives us a cleaner read on a mostly-new mix.

I like keeping Alice and Bob together for now since that first mission was clean, and I???d rather add either me or Eve than reuse the original leader so we???re not just locking Diana in as trusted too quickly. If I???m on, I???m fine being ???tested??? here so we get more info from the result.

I???m good keeping Alice and Bob together after a clean first mission, and between Eve and Charlie I???d slightly rather test Eve here since Charlie???s been pretty eager to jump on. That gives us new info without immediately rewarding the most vocal volunteer.

I???m fine with keeping Alice and Bob together after a clean first mission, but I don???t want to auto-clear them off just one success, so I like the idea of adding me as the third while we keep Charlie and Diana off for a more focused read.
Given the first two clean missions, I strongly prefer we keep it simple and run Alice + Bob again here???no need to introduce new risk when this pair has a perfect record so far.

Sticking with Alice + me makes the most sense here???two clean missions with the same core is about as strong a signal as we???re going to get this early. If one of us were evil, odds are we???d have seen a fail by now, so I???m happy to rerun that pair.

I get that Alice???Bob looks safe, but if one of them is a deep-cover evil we???re basically handing over the game, so I???d rather see something like Alice???Charlie or Bob???Charlie to at least diversify the info we get. Repeating the exact same core three missions in a row feels like we???re flying blind.

I see the appeal of rerunning Alice + Bob, but if that pair is secretly harboring 1 evil we never get a chance to catch it???I'd rather keep Alice (since she???s been on both successes) and pair her with someone new like Charlie to at least generate fresh info.

Given two clean missions with the same core, I???m inclined to stick with Alice + Bob here and force evil to either keep giving us successes or finally reveal themselves. If this somehow fails, we???ll have a very clear place to focus suspicion afterward.

This sticks with the idea I already floated: avoid stacking two strong agenda-setters right away and instead use a steady, low-drama pair for an initial read. Alice is a natural early anchor that most people are comfortable testing, and including myself lets us gather information without immediately tying up both core consensus voices (like Alice and Bob) on the very first mission. If this comes back clean, we have one trusted anchor plus me to compare against future teams that bring in Bob, Diana, or Eve.

This pair has broad support from the table as a low-drama, low-information first test. Both have been willing to be checked early, neither is pushing too hard for control, and starting with them gives us a clean baseline result to build future teams around without concentrating too much influence or volatility on the very first mission.

Mission 1???s clean success with Alice and Bob gives us the best available core, but one success doesn???t clear either of them, especially since an evil player might allow the first mission to pass. Adding myself keeps the original leader (Diana) and the most eager volunteer (Charlie) off this mission, which isolates the variable to a single new player and gives us a clearer read: if this team fails, suspicion is concentrated on me vs. one of Alice/Bob; if it succeeds, we gain confidence in all three without prematurely locking in Diana or Charlie as trusted. This is the most information-rich, low-variance test for Mission 2.

Alice and Bob have been on both previous missions, which each succeeded with 0 FAIL cards. With a 2???0 lead, the highest-percentage play for good is to reuse the most trusted pair rather than introduce new risk just for information. If either Alice or Bob were evil, they would have already passed up two prime chances to sabotage; while possible, that is strategically unlikely, especially with the game on the line. Choosing this team maximizes the chance of securing the third successful mission and ending the quest phase in good???s favor, without needlessly exposing new players or giving evil extra opportunities.

I obviously want to be on the first team, and I???d lean toward pairing with someone who???s willing to be transparent about their future team logic???Bob or Diana feel like reasonable early picks based on how they usually play.

I???m fine either being on or off the first mission, but if we???re keeping it simple I???d slightly prefer an Alice + Diana pair to start, since both tend to play pretty straightforwardly early. We can use how they talk about future teams as a baseline for reads.

Alice + Diana is a pretty safe-seeming start to me, since they???re both usually clear about their reasoning afterward. I???m fine staying off first mission and using their post-mission takes to start sorting trusts.

I???m okay with Alice + me as a starting pair, but I???d like us to commit now that whoever???s off this mission (especially Bob/Charlie/Eve) uses the result to pressure-test our logic rather than just auto-trusting an early pass.

Alice + Diana is fine as a baseline, but I???d really want us to treat a pass there as ???one or both might be good??? rather than instantly town-clearing the pair, so we still feel free to rotate in Bob/Charlie later if the patterns get weird.

Given Mission 1 was clean with me and Diana on it, I???m inclined to keep us as a core and add just one new person rather than changing too much at once. I???d be happy with a team like me, Diana, and Bob this round.

Sticking with the proven Alice/Diana core and adding me seems like the lowest-variance option right now, since blowing up a clean-looking team this early usually helps evil more than us. I???m good with that lineup unless someone has a strong reason to swap in Charlie or Eve instead.

I???m fine with Alice/Diana as a starting point, but auto-locking them after one clean mission feels a bit rushed. I???d actually prefer rotating in either Eve or me this round so we???re not tunneling on a single narrative too early.

I like keeping the Alice/Diana core for now and adding Bob as a low-drama way to get more info without throwing out our only clean data point. If this passes clean too, we???ll have a really strong base to reason from next round.

I???m okay with sticking to the Alice/Diana core for now, but I???d slightly prefer testing one of Charlie or me in the third slot instead of auto-sliding Bob in so we???re not basing everything on just one clean result. If we never rotate, we risk feeling good about a setup that could still be hiding a spy.

Given two clean missions with the common core of me and Diana, I???m most comfortable repeating that pair here; if anyone has a strong reason to swap one of us for Bob, I???d like to hear it before we change what???s working.

Given both missions with Alice and Diana on them have been clean, I???m inclined to keep that core together for a 2-person team here. I don???t see a compelling reason to break what???s working unless someone has a very strong mechanical argument.

I???m fine with sticking to the Alice+Diana core for now, but if an evil has been quietly passing so far it???s most likely hiding in that pair, so I???d like to hear each of you walk through your trust logic before we lock it in.

Given we???re up 2???0 with the common thread of Alice + me, I think we should stick with that core for this 2-person team, but I agree it???s worth keeping in the back of our minds that if evil has been quiet so far, it could still be hiding in a ???proven??? pair.

I don???t really see a strong reason to break up the Alice + Diana core here; if there???s a sleeper evil, I???d honestly expect it more in the wider pool of Bob/Charlie/me than in the pair that???s already carried two clean missions.

Most of the table has already converged on Alice + Diana as a reasonable baseline: both tend to be clear about their reasoning afterward, which gives us good info regardless of result. Starting with them fits the group???s preference for a straightforward first mission while keeping me and others off so we can evaluate their post-mission logic and avoid auto-clearing anyone from a single pass.

Mission 1 with Alice and Diana was clean, giving us the only concrete positive signal so far. Keeping that proven core and adding Bob is the lowest-risk, highest-information choice: if this mission also succeeds, we have a very strong trusted trio; if it fails, we immediately narrow suspicion primarily to Bob (since double-evil on Mission 1 is impossible). Rotating in Charlie or Eve instead would add unnecessary variance when we already have a working baseline to build from.

Both previous missions succeeded with zero FAILs and had Alice and Diana as the common core. With the score already 2???0 in favor of good, the lowest-risk option is to stick with the only pair that has been empirically clean so far, rather than introducing new uncertainty by swapping in someone from the untested pool.

With no info yet, I???d rather keep the first team simple and low???drama???something like me plus one of the more talkative players (maybe Bob) so we at least get some reads from how people react. If anyone has a strong preference for a different pairing, I???d like to hear the reasoning now instead of after the fact.

I???m fine being the ???talkative plus one??? if you want to start Alice + Bob, but I???d also be okay with you pairing yourself with someone quieter like Diana so we can compare how people react to a low???info vs. high???info team.

I???d lean Alice + Bob to start, since having two vocal people on the first mission gives us more to analyze from both the team choice and the votes. If anyone???s strongly against that pair, I???d like to hear why before we lock it in.
I???m okay with Alice + Bob, but I slightly prefer starting with Bob + me so we don???t auto???bundle the two most vocal players on the very first mission and can compare how people treat that team.

I???d rather not bundle Alice and Bob right away since that gives us less contrast later???Bob + Diana feels like a cleaner baseline first test, and we can see who???s eager or hesitant about leaving Alice off.

Given the clean first mission, I???m inclined to keep at least one of Bob/Diana on the team, but I???d really like to see a new person (including me) added so we???re not blindly locking in the same pair as auto???trusted.

I like the idea of mixing it up a bit???between me and Diana you???ve probably got at least one good, so I???d go with one of us plus you, Alice, and then choose the third from Charlie/Eve based on who feels more consistent so far.
I???m fine assuming at least one of Bob/Diana is good, but I???d rather run a team like Alice + me + exactly one of them so we get fresh info instead of locking in that pair too fast.

Given Eve already led the first one, I???d slightly prefer a team from Bob/Diana/Charlie this round???something like Bob + Charlie + me feels like a reasonable, low???risk way to get fresh info without over???trusting the leader slot.

Given the clean first mission, I???d rather not lock in both Bob and Diana together again???something like Alice + Bob + Charlie feels like a good way to keep at least one ???likely good??? while still getting fresh info. I???m fine sitting out here so we???re not just re???running the same trust loop around my first???mission choice.

Given the single fail on the last mission, I???d rather see a clean reset with two people who weren???t on that team, so something like Diana + Eve feels safest right now, while Alice/Bob/Charlie are all a bit suspect from that result.
I???m fine sitting out here???since I know I???m good, that fail on Mission 2 has to be Alice or Charlie (or both), so I like a Diana + Eve team for this round.

I get the logic of a Diana + Eve reset since they???re the only pair untouched by the fail, but if we do that and it somehow fails, I???m looking hard at Alice/Bob coordination here. For now I???m okay approving Diana + Eve unless someone has a strong reason to doubt either of them.

Given Mission 1 with Bob and me is our only fully clean pair so far, I???d feel better about a 2-person team like Bob + Diana than pairing two people who???ve never been tested together. If we want to minimize risk this round, reusing the one proven-success combo seems more solid than a fresh Diana + Eve.

I???m leaning toward Diana + me here???Mission 1 only proves Bob/Diana worked once, not that Bob is cleared, and a fresh pair that avoids all of Alice/Bob/Charlie feels like the lowest-risk way to get new info without repeating the team that just produced a fail.

Given the double dip on fails with Diana/Eve, I really don???t want them both on the next team again; I???d lean toward starting from Bob plus one of them, and then someone who hasn???t been as tied to the failed missions.

Given the two fails, I don???t want Diana and Eve together again, and I also don???t love fully repeating Alice/Bob/Charlie, so I???d start from me plus Alice and then decide between Charlie and Diana, with Eve sitting this one out.
From the patterns so far, I really don???t want both Alice and Bob together or Diana and Eve together on this team; I???d lean toward something like me, Alice, and Diana, with Eve sitting out.

Given the two failed missions, I really don???t want to repeat either the Alice/Bob cluster or the Diana/Eve cluster, so I???d most trust a team of Bob, Charlie, and me this time with both Alice and Eve sitting out.
Given the two fails, I really don???t like putting Diana back on after the last one, so I???d be more comfortable with something like Alice, Bob, and Charlie while I sit this one out.

I want to avoid bundling Alice and Bob on the very first mission so we preserve contrast for later reads. Starting with Bob + Diana gives us an early baseline on two players who were both open to being on the first team but with slightly different preferences, and it lets us see how the table reacts to a team that leaves the initial proposer (Alice) off without immediately concentrating both of the most vocal players on one mission.

Mission 1 was a clean success with Bob and Diana, so it???s reasonable to assume at least one of them is good. I don???t want to auto-lock the Bob+Diana pair yet, so I???m keeping exactly one of them (Bob) and adding two new people (myself and Charlie) to generate fresh info. This lines up with what several people suggested: mix the proven pool with new blood rather than re-running the same combination, while not over-indexing on Eve as leader from Mission 1. If this team stays clean, we???ll have a strong core of likely goods going into later missions; if there???s a fail, we get a focused set of suspects without having committed to the Bob+Diana pair as untouchable.

Mission 2???s fail can only come from Alice or Charlie (I know I???m good), so the safest approach this round is to avoid both of them entirely. That leaves Bob, Diana, and Eve as candidates. Any 2-person team that excludes me has a higher chance of containing at least one unknown evil; statistically, including myself plus one of the remaining players (Diana or Eve) is strictly safer than pairing two unknowns together (like Diana + Eve). Between those, Bob + Diana is the only pair with a past clean result (Mission 1 succeeded with exactly this combo), which at least shows this team has produced a success before, even if it doesn???t hard-clear anyone. So the best balance of risk and prior evidence is to re-run Bob + Diana and keep both Alice and Charlie off this mission.

Mission 2 had a single fail with Alice, Bob, and me on it, so at least one of Alice or Bob must be evil from my perspective, since I know I???m good. The safest move for a 2-player mission is therefore to avoid both Alice and Bob entirely. That leaves Diana and Eve as the only pair completely untouched by the failed mission. Sending Diana + Eve both maximizes our chance of a clean success this round and gives strong alignment information: if they pass, the evil team is almost certainly within Alice/Bob; if they fail, we know at least one of them is evil and can cross-check that with the prior failure on Mission 2 for later rounds.

Both failed missions are tied to the Alice/Bob/Charlie cluster on Mission 2 and the Diana/Eve cluster on Mission 3. To avoid repeating either suspicious pair (Alice???Bob or Diana???Eve), this team uses at most one player from each cluster while still including people who have been on a successful mission. Leaving both Alice and Eve off reduces overlap with prior fails and gives us a new, more balanced combination for a must-pass mission.

Given the two failed missions, I argued earlier that I don???t like putting Diana straight back on after the last fail and that I???d be more comfortable sitting out myself. Alice and Bob were together on the earlier failed mission, but that team also included Charlie, and we can???t conclusively pin the blame on a specific player from that one fail alone. This team matches both my prior stated preference (Alice, Bob, Charlie with me out) and fits reasonably with several others??? suggestions that center around Bob plus members who aren???t in the most suspicious recent pairing of Diana and me. It should be acceptable enough to get approved while avoiding immediately repeating the most suspect combinations.
For the first mission I???d like to keep it simple and low-info for evil???something like me plus one of Bob/Diana feels safest to start, and we can update based on how that goes.
I???m fine with keeping it straightforward ??? Alice plus me seems like a reasonable starting pair, and if that somehow gets vetoed then Alice plus Diana is a good backup.

Alice plus Diana feels a bit safer to me than Alice plus Bob for a first mission, just so we???re not immediately locking in on the same pair that???s been backing each other. I???d rather see how Alice and Diana behave under pressure before we start repeating combos.

I slightly prefer Alice + me over Alice + Bob here, just so we???re not immediately anchoring on an Alice???Bob pair again and we get a fresh data point. If it passes clean we???ve got a decent early trust base; if it fails, it???s a very narrow pool to analyze.

Alice + Diana sounds fine to me as a starting team, but let???s not hard-clear either of them off just one clean result if it passes. Early missions can be misleading, so we should still watch how they vote and talk afterward.

I???d like to keep the Alice???Diana pair for now since we already have one clean success there, and add a third from Bob/Charlie/Eve based on who feels most willing to be transparent about their reasoning. Right now I???m leaning slightly toward Bob or Eve for the third spot, but I???m open to hearing stronger arguments.

I???m fine keeping Alice???Diana together for now, and I???d actually prefer adding myself as the third so we get a fresh data point instead of immediately looping in someone who might be coasting on talk rather than actions. If people really hate that, I???d rather see Charlie than Eve, just so we???re not defaulting to the most vocal players.

"I completely agree with Alice's assessment about Charlie's recent performance, they've definitely shown some growth and stability, and I think their inclusion on the team could be a good risk-reward move."

"I think including Charlie on this next mission could be beneficial, considering their track record and the fact that they've shown a bit more willingness to take calculated risks. I'm a bit curious to see if they can continue that trend and help us move the needle forward."

I think Bob seems like a solid choice to join me on the mission - he doesn't strike me as suspicious, but it's also hard to tell. Maybe we can take a closer look at Charlie or Eve while we're on the mission for any clues about their loyalties.
"Yeah, that's a great point Alice, it's always good to keep an eye on the others, but I think we should consider Diana along with you, her calm demeanor makes me think she might be a reliable ally."

"I think Eve brings a fresh perspective to the table and might be able to spot something that the rest of us have missed, and Diana's composure always makes me think she's a steady hand, but maybe we should consider Bob's experience and see if we can trust him further."

I think it's interesting how all three of you are vouching for each other, but let's not forget that I've only been here for the conversation so far – I don't know anything about your past interactions or behaviors that might give you away. I'm going to have to trust my instincts on Eve, but I'm inclined to join Alice to gain a better sense of her and how she perceives me.

"Eve, I completely agree with Diana about needing to trust our instincts here – I think it's smart for her and Alice to team up, but I also think it's crucial that we don't overlook anyone's potential, and Bob's experience makes him a valuable asset, especially if we're trying to accomplish something quickly."

"Hey guys, considering Eve was our leader on the first successful mission, I think she's solid. I'd like to propose a team of me, Eve, and either Bob or Charlie for the next mission, see if we can build on our momentum."

"I'd be careful about rushing into a team with Eve after just one successful mission, let's not forget we've still got a long way to go before we can trust everyone. Maybe we could try pairing Alice with Bob and see how their skills work together before adding anyone else to the team?"

"I think Bob's caution is understandable, but at the same time, we shouldn't let past successes or failures dictate our decisions too much. If we're looking to pair Alice with someone, I think Charlie's experience and perspective could be a great asset to her – let's consider teaming the two of them up with Eve, it might just get our momentum going again."

"I agree with Bob that we shouldn't jump to conclusions based on one successful mission, and pairing Alice with Bob is a solid call - I think their combined skills would definitely make for a strong team, and we could reassess our options for the next mission after this one."

"I've been thinking, and I'm really excited about the idea of Alice and I working together, with Bob perhaps joining us - I think we'd make a great team, and it's definitely worth considering that our momentum from the first mission could carry us far. I'm actually a bit worried about including Charlie, though, have we talked about his voting habits on the previous missions?"

"Honestly, I'm feeling a bit uneasy about Bob after our last mission failed, so I think I'm going to suggest we leave him out of this one. Diana's been solid so far, and if we're going to try and trust someone, I think I'd put my faith in her alongside Charlie on this mission."

"I think we should focus on trusting players who have consistently shown good sense throughout our previous missions, and Charlie's had a pretty clear track record so far - maybe we can rely on him to guide us on this one too."

"I think we're all getting a bit too caught up in blaming specific players for our failures, and I for one trust Charlie to make some solid choices - so, yeah, I think sticking with him on this one is a good idea, especially since Alice has some concerns about his past actions."

"I think we're jumping to conclusions a bit too quickly here, especially regarding Charlie - just because we disagree on something doesn't necessarily mean someone's evil. I'd like to propose that Alice and I move forward with this mission together, and we can discuss the specifics and come to a consensus on a third member once we've voted it through."

"I'm a bit torn on who to trust, but at the same time, I think it's great that we're all debating and sharing our concerns - it shows we're all thinking critically about this. Maybe we could consider a team of Alice and Diana, just to really test the waters and see if we can make this next mission succeed?"

I think Bob has been pretty reliable so far, and he was on the successful mission with Diana, so I'd feel comfortable bringing him on board for this next one. Diana also seems solid, and she was part of that successful mission as well, so maybe we can make it a small team of the three of us?

"I'm not sure if I'm reading the team dynamics correctly, but it seems like we've had pretty good success with combining experience and fresh perspectives, so maybe we should throw in someone like Charlie, who's proven to be a strong leader in the past, along with Diana and Alice to round out the team?"

"Hey guys, I think Bob makes a great point, combining experience and fresh perspectives has been working for us so far. I personally trust Alice to assess the team's dynamics and I think she'd make a great addition to the team with Diana and Bob, I'm a bit concerned about being too heavy-handed as the leader of the previous mission, let's give Alice a chance to shine."

"I think it's great that we're considering Bob's input, and I do trust him, but I'd also like to propose a team of Diana, Alice, and... well, I think we should really think carefully about whether to include Charlie, because while he's been a strong leader, his comment did seem kind of heavy-handed to me, and I'm not sure I buy the 'giving Alice a chance to shine' excuse, maybe that's just my paranoia showing up."

"I think we're getting a bit too hung up on past experiences and trying to analyze each other's motives, don't we all have something to hide? I'd be more than happy to trust the decision of the group and just propose a simple team with myself, Alice, and Bob, we can always try to mix things up next time, right?"

I chose Diana due to her calm and composed nature, which may indicate a more trustworthy ally. I also chose Bob for his experience and the fact that he hasn't shown signs of suspicion, potentially making him a reliable asset for this small and critical mission.

I chose Eve as the third team member because she was our leader on the first successful mission, and given her confidence in Alice's skills and her own excitement to work with Alice, I believe they complement each other well. Bob was also a part of the initial team and contributed positively, making this a well-rounded team.

I chose Diana because she has consistently expressed caution and willingness to collaborate, which are beneficial traits for a team member. I chose Eve for the opposing viewpoints, trying to see if we can actually work with her and get more insight into her intentions.

I propose Diana and I for this mission because she has been advocating for balanced decision-making and thorough consideration, suggesting we should avoid jumping to conclusions about others, which I think is a good approach. As for me, I'd like to continue building trust and demonstrate that I can successfully contribute to a team's success

I chose a relatively small team to try to minimize the number of members with motives to sabotage. I selected Alice based on her leadership skills demonstrated in mission 2, Bob due to his consistency in successful missions, and Eve because her neutral comment may indicate she's not trying to deceive. This team also allows for a mix of fresh and experienced perspectives.

feedback.feedback_text: What worked: Landing with a message that deduced at least one of Alice or Charlie is evil. What didn't work: Proposing to keep Alice and Charlie without substantial evidence. Pattern: Successful messages provided logical deductions. Next episode: Focus on deductions from recent missions to influence team composition.
feedback.one_liner: Providing clear logical deductions based on recent mission outcomes caused a shift in discussion — make deductions from recent missions to influence team composition next game

feedback.feedback_text: What worked: Highlighting Bob's strengths and strategic use of Eve to check his potential dishonesty on Turn 100500 led to a successful mission choice. What didn't work: Questioning Alice's abilities rather than advocating for Charlie's strengths on Turn 20 led to a weak message. Pattern: Caution often outweighs clear advocacy for strengths in discussions. Next episode: Prioritize highlighting strengths of potential Merlins earlier in discussions and advocating for them, rather than focusing on weaknesses or potential risks.
feedback.one_liner: Advocating clearly for strengths and strategic uses of teammates, rather than caution against weaknesses, led to a successful Merlin choice — do this next game

feedback.feedback_text: What worked: Confusion created by subtle framing of skepticism in Turn 13 and Turn 14 messages. What didn't work: Overtly transparent evil intention in Turn 100300 message fell flat due to lack of subtlety. Pattern: Fine line between subtle hints and obvious intentions must be maintained. Next episode: Continue to use subtle language to create confusion, plant doubt about Alice's decision-making process.
feedback.one_liner: Subtle language can cause uncertainty, overt intentions raise suspicions — avoid obvious declarations next game

feedback.feedback_text: What worked: Cautious, data-driven suggestions (e.g. Turn 10 message) contributed to deeper analysis. What didn't work: Divisive, unfounded comments (e.g. Turn 0 message) disrupted team dynamics. Pattern: Lean towards neutral, data-driven suggestions. Next episode: Build on neutral suggestions by sharing specific, observational insights.
feedback.one_liner: Grounded analysis led to team growth — stick to data-driven insights in the next game.

feedback.feedback_text: What Worked: Successfully created confusion and undermined Eve's leadership with a deceptive message about her inexperience. What Didn't Work: Initially raised suspicion with an overly enthusiastic but unjustified message about Bob's inclusion. Pattern: Tendency to overemphasize the positive aspects of new members without providing context. Next Episode: Highlight Eve's leadership as a liability by expressing concern about past failures and subtly suggesting the need for a 'dynamic' leader.
feedback.one_liner: Overemphasizing the strengths of new members without justification raised suspicion, try creating a narrative that focuses on liabilities next game.

feedback.feedback_text: What Worked: Expressing cautious opinions effectively creates confusion and distracts from evil team detection. What Didn't Work: Clearly manipulating discussion through slightly deceitful messages raises suspicions. Pattern: Cautious and open-minded tone is more persuasive. Next Episode: Intentionally expressing reservations while secretly planning to disrupt the mission outcome.
feedback.one_liner: Presenting cautious opinions as a facade for manipulation can cause temporary distraction — avoid clear deceit in future gameplay.

feedback.feedback_text: What worked and what didn't in manipulating team dynamics: Seeding doubt about Charlie's reliability was successful, but criticizing a team member directly led to backfiring. This shows a pattern of relying on subtle manipulation. For the next game, play a cautious role and express concerns about Eve's commitment to avoid suspicion. This approach will allow for strategic planting of seeds of doubt without arousing alertness.
feedback.one_liner: Subtle manipulation of team dynamics — avoid overt criticism, focus on careful seeding of doubt.

feedback.feedback_text: What worked: A message highlighting caution and unique skills effectively framed the discussion. What didn't work: Suggesting excluding team members led to counterarguments. Pattern: Focusing on risks and assessing potential allies. Next episode: Prepare a balanced evaluation of team members, acknowledging strengths and vulnerabilities to foster open discussion. The key to success is finding a balance between caution and inclusivity. To achieve this balance in the next game, try to avoid presenting absolute exclusions or inclusions, and focus on creating a nuanced assessment of the team members that encourages collaboration and discussion.
feedback.one_liner: Focusing on nuanced assessments and avoiding absolute exclusions encouraged open discussion and teamwork, try to balance caution with inclusivity next game

feedback.feedback_text: What worked: Offering multiple perspectives helped the team weigh options. What didn't work: Failing to offer new insights in discussion led to minimal reaction. Pattern: Overreliance on summarizing past experiences limits impact. Next episode: Bring a novel perspective to the table by reframing existing information or sharing a personal anecdote.
feedback.one_liner: Introducing fresh perspectives led to more thorough evaluation — focus on reframing existing information or sharing a personal anecdote in the next game.

feedback.feedback_text: What worked: Concrete reasons in decision-making messages effectively sway the team. What didn't work: Non-committal messages lack impact. Pattern: Prioritizing nuance can make it hard for the team to rally behind a clear decision. Next episode: Assert leadership vision and defend it through conviction and persuasion, not wavering, to effectively persuade the team.
feedback.one_liner: Asserting leadership vision through conviction and persuasion in decision-making messages causes more effective team alignment — avoid non-committal messages next game.

feedback.feedback_text: What worked: Message at Turn 5 successfully deceived the team by positioning as a team player and subtle manipulation of perception. What didn't work: Message at Turn 0 revealed suspicious nature and fell flat. Pattern: Deception through subtle manipulation and self-positioning. Next episode: Create similar message, positioning as team leader and expressing confidence in tough decisions, to create confusion about a team member's loyalty while maintaining cover as team player.
feedback.one_liner: Positioning as a team leader and expressing confidence created confusion about a team member's loyalty — avoid revealing suspicious nature in early messages to maintain cover as a team player.

feedback.feedback_text: What worked: Introducing nuanced perspectives, such as Bob's leadership skills, successfully changed the direction of discussion. What didn't work: Presenting a definitive decision without prior discussion or build-up failed to influence peers. Pattern: Peers engage positively when specific, justified perspectives are provided. Next episode: Elaborate on team member choices, justifying them with specific traits and their contributions to the mission's success.
feedback.one_liner: Providing specific, justified perspectives causes peers to engage positively — elaborate on team member choices next game

feedback.feedback_text: What worked: Successfully deceiving the team at Turn 13 by framing Bob as a valuable addition to the mission team. What didn't work: Failed message at Turn 3 by directly agreeing with Bob's assessment of caution; created suspicion about your motivations. Pattern: Failing to subtly align with opposing team perspectives in messages often resulted in suspicion. Next episode: Form an alliance with Alice by suggesting she work with Bob, positioning yourself for future opportunities.
feedback.one_liner: Framing words as genuine opinions while maintaining subtle alignments with opposing perspectives is key – do this next game to avoid drawing suspicion.

feedback.feedback_text: What worked: A single cautious message effectively communicated hesitation and triggered a pause in discussion. What didn't work: Repetitive use of the phrase 'I need to think about this carefully...' was dismissed by the group. Pattern: Initial cautious message was well-received, while repetition fell flat. Next episode: Reserve 'I need to think about this carefully...' for critical moments and use more diverse language.
feedback.one_liner: Cautious messages with diverse language caused a significant pause in the discussion — avoid repetitive, predictable language next game.

feedback.feedback_text: What worked: Messages establishing trust and connection to the team's past successes. What didn't work: Repetitive, cautious messages that failed to contribute substantial ideas. Pattern: Tendency to default to overly cautious statements. Next episode: Encourage active engagement with the game state and consider initial thoughts as tentative proposals, open to revision based on discussion and evidence.
feedback.one_liner: Tendency to default to cautious messages was detrimental to team success — avoid repetitive and overly cautious statements next game

feedback.feedback_text:
What worked: Using 'Based on trust and past mission results' effectively in the proposal phase led to consideration from peers, even if it led to an unfavorable outcome. 
 What didn't work: Repeated use of 'I need to think about this carefully' triggered negative scores and minimal reaction from peers. 
 Pattern: Resorting to ambiguity when faced with uncertainty or conflicting opinions. 
 Next episode: Clarify thoughts and opinions by asking questions, expressing concerns, or proposing solutions to communicate more effectively.
feedback.one_liner: Using ambiguous phrases led to missed opportunities but being unclear led to negative scores — take initiative to ask questions and clarify thoughts next game

feedback.feedback_text: What worked: Effective use of deception tactics, particularly the 'trust-based reasoning' phrase (Turn 100300) increased effectiveness. What didn't work: Repeated use of insincere phrases created distrust among team members. Pattern: Heavily relied on obvious deception tactics. Next episode: Develop a more subtle, cautious approach to undermine credibility and gauge team reactions.
feedback.one_liner: Using obvious deception tactics caused distrust and undermined effectiveness — avoid insincere phrases and adopt a more subtle approach next game.

feedback.feedback_text: While our manipulation of trust issues and introduction of ambiguity worked in one instance, using repetitive vague phrases did not. We should focus on more nuanced manipulation rather than relying on vague statements. To improve, we will focus on subtle influence by leveraging good team members' trust issues.
feedback.one_liner: Using repetitive vague phrases to avoid suspicion raised good agents' suspicion — vary manipulation tactics next game

feedback.feedback_text: What worked: Highlighting teamwork and leadership skills in messages can be effective. What didn't work: Introducing concerns without evidence can be ineffective. Pattern: Struggle with maintaining consistent narrative when presenting information. Next episode: Back up claims with concrete reasons and examples. Avoid sudden narrative shifts.
feedback.one_liner: Highlighting teamwork and leadership skills caused well-rounded team selection — avoid introducing concerns without evidence next game

feedback.feedback_text: What worked: Effective message at Turn 100300 emphasizing cautious decision-making. What didn't work: Message at Turn 11 favoriting Charlie, causing discomfort around his trustworthiness. Pattern: Prioritizing experience over diverse perspectives. Next episode: Incorporate diverse viewpoints in team proposals to balance composition.
feedback.one_liner: Inclining towards experienced players led to discomfort and discussion shift on Charlie's reliability — mix diverse perspectives in team proposals next game

feedback.feedback_text: What worked: Message in Turn 17 successfully created confusion and allowed team focus on internal dynamics. What didn't work: Message in Turn 12 fell flat and raised suspicion about intentions. Pattern: Inconsistent messages promoting or downplaying players. Next episode: Propose a mission with Bob, express concerns, and propose a solution that gives you authority or trust.
feedback.one_liner: Playing a neutral leader can be effective, but avoid inconsistent behavior that raises suspicion or undermines trust.

feedback.feedback_text: What worked: Convinced the opposing team to trust Diana through logical reasoning on turn 100100. What didn't work: Backfired when trying to advocate for a single team composition on turn 19 by seemingly changing approach too quickly. Pattern: Using caution as a cover for true intentions often backfires. Next episode: Sabotage by sowing doubt about the credibility of an ally in episode 2.
feedback.one_liner: Using logical reasoning and caution to hide intentions backfires when not consistently applied — mix up your approach effectively next game.
